────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  optiqor   Helm chart cost & security analysis
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  source: /home/shivam/Desktop/optiqor/optiqor-cli/testdata/fixtures/basic-chart/values.yaml
  15 workloads · 49 findings

   HIGH   admin-tool
    allowPrivilegeEscalation explicitly enabled
    securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation=true lets a process gain
    capabilities its parent didn't have (setuid, file capabilities). Set
    allowPrivilegeEscalation=false unless you're running an init system or
    known-good legacy binary that requires it.
    confidence: ●●● high

   HIGH   admin-tool
    Container declared privileged
    securityContext.privileged=true bypasses every Linux capability check.
    Privileged is correct only for node-level agents (CSI drivers, node
    exporters); application workloads should set privileged=false and request
    the specific capabilities they actually need.
    confidence: ●●● high

   HIGH   admin-tool
    Container runs as root
    securityContext.runAsNonRoot is set to false. Running as UID 0 expands the
    blast radius of any container escape. Set runAsNonRoot=true and runAsUser
    to a non-zero UID.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    admin-tool
    capabilities.drop does not include ALL
    Best-practice baseline (CIS 5.2.8) is securityContext.capabilities.drop:
    [ALL] with add: [...] re-granting only the specific capabilities required.
    The current chart does not drop ALL, so the container inherits the default
    capability set including CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID, NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    admin-tool
    ServiceAccount token automatically mounted
    automountServiceAccountToken is undeclared, so Kubernetes auto-mounts a
    service-account credential at
    /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. Most application workloads
    never read it; an attacker on the pod gets a free pivot to the K8s API.
    Set automountServiceAccountToken=false unless the workload genuinely calls
    the API.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    api
    allowPrivilegeEscalation not declared
    securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation is undeclared; Kubernetes
    defaults it to true. Set allowPrivilegeEscalation=false to make the
    no_new_privs bit explicit.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    api
    capabilities.drop does not include ALL
    Best-practice baseline (CIS 5.2.8) is securityContext.capabilities.drop:
    [ALL] with add: [...] re-granting only the specific capabilities required.
    The current chart does not drop ALL, so the container inherits the default
    capability set including CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID, NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    api
    runAsNonRoot not declared
    securityContext.runAsNonRoot is not declared. Without it, Kubernetes
    defers to the image's USER directive — which is UID 0 for most public
    images. Set runAsNonRoot=true to make the intent explicit.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    api
    ServiceAccount token automatically mounted
    automountServiceAccountToken is undeclared, so Kubernetes auto-mounts a
    service-account credential at
    /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. Most application workloads
    never read it; an attacker on the pod gets a free pivot to the K8s API.
    Set automountServiceAccountToken=false unless the workload genuinely calls
    the API.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   LOW    api
    readOnlyRootFilesystem not declared
    securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem is not declared. Declare
    readOnlyRootFilesystem=true to make hardening explicit; mount tmpfs
    volumes for legitimate write paths.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    bursty
    capabilities.drop does not include ALL
    Best-practice baseline (CIS 5.2.8) is securityContext.capabilities.drop:
    [ALL] with add: [...] re-granting only the specific capabilities required.
    The current chart does not drop ALL, so the container inherits the default
    capability set including CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID, NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    bursty
    ServiceAccount token automatically mounted
    automountServiceAccountToken is undeclared, so Kubernetes auto-mounts a
    service-account credential at
    /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. Most application workloads
    never read it; an attacker on the pod gets a free pivot to the K8s API.
    Set automountServiceAccountToken=false unless the workload genuinely calls
    the API.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    cache
    allowPrivilegeEscalation not declared
    securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation is undeclared; Kubernetes
    defaults it to true. Set allowPrivilegeEscalation=false to make the
    no_new_privs bit explicit.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    cache
    capabilities.drop does not include ALL
    Best-practice baseline (CIS 5.2.8) is securityContext.capabilities.drop:
    [ALL] with add: [...] re-granting only the specific capabilities required.
    The current chart does not drop ALL, so the container inherits the default
    capability set including CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID, NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    cache
    Image not pinned to a stable tag
    Image redis:latest uses :latest. The same tag can resolve to different
    bytes across deploys; rollback becomes ambiguous. Pin to an immutable
    digest or version tag.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    cache
    ServiceAccount token automatically mounted
    automountServiceAccountToken is undeclared, so Kubernetes auto-mounts a
    service-account credential at
    /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. Most application workloads
    never read it; an attacker on the pod gets a free pivot to the K8s API.
    Set automountServiceAccountToken=false unless the workload genuinely calls
    the API.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   LOW    cache
    readOnlyRootFilesystem not declared
    securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem is not declared. Declare
    readOnlyRootFilesystem=true to make hardening explicit; mount tmpfs
    volumes for legitimate write paths.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    dev-build
    capabilities.drop does not include ALL
    Best-practice baseline (CIS 5.2.8) is securityContext.capabilities.drop:
    [ALL] with add: [...] re-granting only the specific capabilities required.
    The current chart does not drop ALL, so the container inherits the default
    capability set including CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID, NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    dev-build
    ServiceAccount token automatically mounted
    automountServiceAccountToken is undeclared, so Kubernetes auto-mounts a
    service-account credential at
    /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. Most application workloads
    never read it; an attacker on the pod gets a free pivot to the K8s API.
    Set automountServiceAccountToken=false unless the workload genuinely calls
    the API.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   LOW    dev-build
    readOnlyRootFilesystem not declared
    securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem is not declared. Declare
    readOnlyRootFilesystem=true to make hardening explicit; mount tmpfs
    volumes for legitimate write paths.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    guaranteed
    ServiceAccount token automatically mounted
    automountServiceAccountToken is undeclared, so Kubernetes auto-mounts a
    service-account credential at
    /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. Most application workloads
    never read it; an attacker on the pod gets a free pivot to the K8s API.
    Set automountServiceAccountToken=false unless the workload genuinely calls
    the API.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    logger
    allowPrivilegeEscalation not declared
    securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation is undeclared; Kubernetes
    defaults it to true. Set allowPrivilegeEscalation=false to make the
    no_new_privs bit explicit.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    logger
    capabilities.drop does not include ALL
    Best-practice baseline (CIS 5.2.8) is securityContext.capabilities.drop:
    [ALL] with add: [...] re-granting only the specific capabilities required.
    The current chart does not drop ALL, so the container inherits the default
    capability set including CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID, NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    logger
    Image not pinned to a stable tag
    Image fluentd uses (no tag). The same tag can resolve to different bytes
    across deploys; rollback becomes ambiguous. Pin to an immutable digest or
    version tag.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    logger
    ServiceAccount token automatically mounted
    automountServiceAccountToken is undeclared, so Kubernetes auto-mounts a
    service-account credential at
    /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. Most application workloads
    never read it; an attacker on the pod gets a free pivot to the K8s API.
    Set automountServiceAccountToken=false unless the workload genuinely calls
    the API.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   LOW    logger
    readOnlyRootFilesystem not declared
    securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem is not declared. Declare
    readOnlyRootFilesystem=true to make hardening explicit; mount tmpfs
    volumes for legitimate write paths.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   HIGH   memhog
    Pod shares the host IPC namespace
    hostIPC=true puts the pod in the node's IPC namespace, so it can read and
    write SysV shared memory and POSIX message queues used by other pods on
    the same node. Almost never required; turn it off unless the workload's
    documented dependency demands it.
    confidence: ●●● high

   HIGH   memhog
    Memory limit not set
    This workload declares a memory request but no limit. Without a limit it
    can consume node memory unbounded and evict neighbours under pressure. Set
    a limit at or above your observed P95.
    confidence: ●●● high

   HIGH   ptrace-hunter
    Dangerous Linux capability added
    securityContext.capabilities.add includes 1 escalation-class
    capability(s): SYS_PTRACE (attach to other processes' memory; can read
    secrets out of co-tenant pods). Each grants abilities far beyond what an
    application workload should need. Drop them and reach for a sidecar /
    DaemonSet that runs in a more privileged ServiceAccount if the underlying
    need is legitimate.
    confidence: ●●● high

   HIGH   ptrace-hunter
    Pod shares the host PID namespace
    hostPID=true gives the pod's processes visibility into every other process
    on the node — including kubelet, the container runtime, and every
    co-tenant pod. Required for a tiny set of node-level agents (debuggers,
    profilers); never appropriate for application workloads.
    confidence: ●●● high

   HIGH   ptrace-hunter
    Container runs as root
    securityContext.runAsNonRoot is set to false. Running as UID 0 expands the
    blast radius of any container escape. Set runAsNonRoot=true and runAsUser
    to a non-zero UID.
    confidence: ●●● high

   HIGH   ptrace-hunter
    runAsUser explicitly set to 0
    securityContext.runAsUser is set to 0. This forces the container to run as
    UID 0 (root) regardless of the image's USER directive — a hard override
    that's almost always wrong for application workloads. Set runAsUser to a
    non-zero UID (1000+) and runAsNonRoot=true.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    ptrace-hunter
    allowPrivilegeEscalation not declared
    securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation is undeclared; Kubernetes
    defaults it to true. Set allowPrivilegeEscalation=false to make the
    no_new_privs bit explicit.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    ptrace-hunter
    capabilities.drop does not include ALL
    Best-practice baseline (CIS 5.2.8) is securityContext.capabilities.drop:
    [ALL] with add: [...] re-granting only the specific capabilities required.
    The current chart does not drop ALL, so the container inherits the default
    capability set including CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID, NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.
    confidence: ●●● high

   LOW    ptrace-hunter
    readOnlyRootFilesystem not declared
    securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem is not declared. Declare
    readOnlyRootFilesystem=true to make hardening explicit; mount tmpfs
    volumes for legitimate write paths.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    shipper
    allowPrivilegeEscalation not declared
    securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation is undeclared; Kubernetes
    defaults it to true. Set allowPrivilegeEscalation=false to make the
    no_new_privs bit explicit.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    shipper
    capabilities.drop does not include ALL
    Best-practice baseline (CIS 5.2.8) is securityContext.capabilities.drop:
    [ALL] with add: [...] re-granting only the specific capabilities required.
    The current chart does not drop ALL, so the container inherits the default
    capability set including CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID, NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    shipper
    ServiceAccount token automatically mounted
    automountServiceAccountToken is undeclared, so Kubernetes auto-mounts a
    service-account credential at
    /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. Most application workloads
    never read it; an attacker on the pod gets a free pivot to the K8s API.
    Set automountServiceAccountToken=false unless the workload genuinely calls
    the API.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   LOW    shipper
    readOnlyRootFilesystem not declared
    securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem is not declared. Declare
    readOnlyRootFilesystem=true to make hardening explicit; mount tmpfs
    volumes for legitimate write paths.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   HIGH   sniffer
    Pod uses host network
    hostNetwork=true puts the pod in the node's network namespace, bypassing
    NetworkPolicy and exposing localhost services on the host. Required for
    some CNI plugins and ingress controllers; otherwise leave it unset.
    confidence: ●●● high

   HIGH   sniffer
    Pod mounts a hostPath volume
    A volume of type hostPath exposes the node's filesystem to the pod.
    Required for some node-level agents (log collectors, CNI helpers) but a
    sharp edge for application workloads — a path-traversal bug suddenly
    becomes a node compromise. Replace with PVC, configMap, or downward API
    where possible.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    sniffer
    capabilities.drop does not include ALL
    Best-practice baseline (CIS 5.2.8) is securityContext.capabilities.drop:
    [ALL] with add: [...] re-granting only the specific capabilities required.
    The current chart does not drop ALL, so the container inherits the default
    capability set including CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID, NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    sniffer
    ServiceAccount token automatically mounted
    automountServiceAccountToken is undeclared, so Kubernetes auto-mounts a
    service-account credential at
    /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. Most application workloads
    never read it; an attacker on the pod gets a free pivot to the K8s API.
    Set automountServiceAccountToken=false unless the workload genuinely calls
    the API.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   HIGH   worker
    Memory limit not set
    This workload declares a memory request but no limit. Without a limit it
    can consume node memory unbounded and evict neighbours under pressure. Set
    a limit at or above your observed P95.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    worker
    allowPrivilegeEscalation not declared
    securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation is undeclared; Kubernetes
    defaults it to true. Set allowPrivilegeEscalation=false to make the
    no_new_privs bit explicit.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   MED    worker
    capabilities.drop does not include ALL
    Best-practice baseline (CIS 5.2.8) is securityContext.capabilities.drop:
    [ALL] with add: [...] re-granting only the specific capabilities required.
    The current chart does not drop ALL, so the container inherits the default
    capability set including CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID, NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.
    confidence: ●●● high

   MED    worker
    ServiceAccount token automatically mounted
    automountServiceAccountToken is undeclared, so Kubernetes auto-mounts a
    service-account credential at
    /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. Most application workloads
    never read it; an attacker on the pod gets a free pivot to the K8s API.
    Set automountServiceAccountToken=false unless the workload genuinely calls
    the API.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   LOW    worker
    CPU limit not set
    This workload declares a CPU request but no limit. Some teams omit CPU
    limits intentionally to avoid throttling; others expect predictable
    scheduling. Confirm this is the intended posture.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

   LOW    worker
    readOnlyRootFilesystem not declared
    securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem is not declared. Declare
    readOnlyRootFilesystem=true to make hardening explicit; mount tmpfs
    volumes for legitimate write paths.
    confidence: ●●○ medium

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Sandbox accuracy: ±40%. Install the Optiqor agent for exact numbers (optiqor.dev/get).
  → install the agent for exact numbers: optiqor.dev/get (https://optiqor.dev/get)
